content.full_text:
I(RAMER ASSOCIATES
TIIIRTY-F IRST X'LOOR
1756 BROAD\rAY
NE\l\/ YORK N.Y. 1OO19
(212) 5a2-O3()3
November 26, 1990
Margaret Ellison, Esq.
Senior Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814
Dear Ms. Ellison:
I want to thank you for being so generous with your time in discussing my
situation on Thanksgiving eve. ln accordance with that discussion, lwould
appreciate a judgment by the Commission as to whether I am a "public official"
under Government Code section 82048 and am therelore subject to the
provisions of the Political Reform Act ol 1974.
On July 31 sl, 1990 I signed a contract with the Department of lnsurance
("DOl") to serye as executive director of the California Workers' Compensation
Rate Study Commission ("the Commission"). While I obviously have neither the
expertise nor the authority to interpret CaliJornia's conflict ol interest provisions,
my impression is that the question of whether I am covered turns on two issues.
First, is the Commission itself subject to the contlicts of interest provisions -- that
is, is the Commission a covered agency? Second, assuming it is not, am I
nonetheless a covered public otlicial because the agency that officially
contracted lor my services is the DOl, rather than the Commission? On the
assumption that both issues are relevant, I have atlempted to set forth below
what appear to be the salient facts.
As to the first issue, I believe it is reasonably clear that the Commission
does not have decision making authority as defined by C.C.R., tit. 2, 518700 (a)
(1). lts purpose is to examine California's rate setting mechanism and the
approaches used by other states and to make recommendations to the
Governor, Legislature, and Commissioner of the DOl. lts tindings are purely
advisory, and it cannot make, compel, or prevent a governmental decision.
lrrespective of what the Commission does or does nol recommend, the
Governor and Legislature are free to act as they choose with respecl to
California's rate setting mechanism. Since the Commission has never met and
thus never acted, its recommendations cannot have been "routinely and
regularly followed." lndeed, since the Commission is only requested to make


and will only make one report - the report due June 30, 1991 - whatever
legislalive action is or is not ultimately taken with respect to its lindings will not
be "routine" or "regular. but a one-time response to the findings of a study
commission. Beyond the fact that tho Commission lacks a track record which
would permit a determination that ils conclusions are regularly and routinely
adopted, I do not believe lhere is a legislative expectation thal the lindings of
this panicular Commission will be "routinely" adopted.
As to whether I am a covered person under the Political Relorm Act, the
statute creating the Commission houses it within the DOl. Moreover, lhe DOI is
required to provide the Commission with statfing. Consequenily, my contract
was drawn with the DOl. lt would have been impossible for me to be hired by
the Commission because my early tasks included shaping tho budget and
doing other preliminary groundwork that were undertaken before any
commissioners had been appointed. That is, I had to be hired to begin work to
meet the Commission's deadline, and it was unclear lhat anyone else had the
authority to hire me.
I believe that the statute makes it clear, however, that while the DOI is to
provide "staff and research assistance," the Commission is charged with
undertaking an independent study and reaching independent conclusions,
There is no requirement that the Commission is to report to, consult with, or in
any manner be guided by the Dol. There is no requirement or suggestion that
the Commission should seek the views or approval ol the DOI before submitting
its rindings.
More imporiant, I believe my contract is clear on the point that my role as
executive director is to serve the Commission, not the DOl. My "task summary"
is as lollows: "Serves as Executive Director ol the California Workers'
Compensation Rate Study Commission. Responsible for managing the
operations and activities of the commission.' The more detailed description of
my "lasks and responsibilities" essentially involves "overseeing production ol
such research and background material as the commission may rcquird'lilalics
added). ln short, I believe it is clear that my responsibilities and obligations are
those I owe the commissioners, and that the only role ol the DOI is to assure that
I have performed the tasks required by the Commission.
It would seem that in terms of ihe conllicts of interest provisions, lhe only
distinction between me and the members of the Commission is that because I
am salaried and they are not, I have a contractual relationship with the DOI
which they do not have. Otherwise, I am part of the same non-"decision
making" enterprise. While the DOI is acting as the contracting and paying
agent, functionally I am serving lhe Commission, and the only role for the DOI is
to determine that I have performed my duties to the satislaclion of the
Commission. ln lact, my acceptance of lhe role ol executive director was
conditioned on the understanding thal the Commission would be an
independent enterprise, and that the commissioners would understand that I
was accountable only to them.


I appreciate your attention to this issue. As you know, we have our first
Commission meeting scheduled for December 17th, and I would not want to
have any doubts lingering as to my status. I would have contacted you earlier
had I been aware of the possible application of these provisions to me.
Again, thanks for your effort, and I look foruvard to hearing from you.
Sincerely yours,
tk**
enc.
Orin S.

sections.question: Whether the requestor is a 'public official' under Government Code section 82048 and therefore subject to the provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974, specifically: (1) whether the California Workers' Compensation Rate Study Commission is a covered agency subject to conflict of interest provisions, and (2) whether the requestor is nonetheless a covered public official because the contracting agency is the Department of Insurance rather than the Commission itself.
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor seeks FPPC determination of whether he qualifies as a public official subject to the Political Reform Act based on his role as executive director of an advisory study commission, given that his contract is with the Department of Insurance rather than the Commission itself.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/letter seeking FPPC advice, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or formal advice section is present. Document is dated November 26, 1990 and references a prior Thanksgiving conversation with Margaret Ellison of FPPC. The requestor provides detailed factual background and legal analysis in support of his request.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether the requestor is a 'public official' under Government Code section 82048 and therefore subject to the provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974, specifically: (1) whether the California Workers' Compensation Rate Study Commission is a covered agency subject to conflict of interest provisions, and (2) whether the requestor is nonetheless a covered public official because the contracting agency is the Department of Insurance rather than the Commission itself.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney (Orin S.) seeking FPPC advice on whether his position as executive director of the California Workers' Compensation Rate Study Commission makes him a covered public official under the Political Reform Act of 1974.
